svg.text: LSF — LANGUE DES SIGNES FRANCAISE
NOTATION — PARENT OF MODERN SIGN LANGUAGES
LSF: FRENCH SIGN LANGUAGE
LSF: ~100,000 users in France; also Belgium, Switzerland, Luxembourg
Parent language: LSF is ancestor of ASL, LSM, and influenced DGS
Without LSF, modern Deaf education worldwide would be entirely different
LSF ONE-HANDED MANUAL ALPHABET
LSF: one-handed manual alphabet; same system adopted by ASL in 1817
Laurent Clerc brought LSF alphabet to America; became ASL manual alphabet
LSF alphabet: 26 handshapes + accented letters (e with accent, etc.)
LSF GLOSSING
LSF glossed in French: FEMME DONNER LIVRE = "The woman gives the book"
ELIX: LSF dictionary database; ALIS: French LSF research institute
LSF recognized as official language of France in 2005 (education law)
SAMPLE LSF CHARACTERISTICS
LSF uses face-neutral signing space heavily (more than ASL)
French mouthing: less prominent in LSF than English mouthing in BSL
DGS · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
DGS AND LSF: HISTORICAL SIBLINGS
Both LSF and DGS influenced by Abbe de l'Epee's Paris institute (1760)
German Deaf students exposed to French methods; brought signs to Germany
DGS: more independently developed than ASL from LSF
EUROPEAN SIGN LANGUAGE FAMILY
LSF family: LSF, ASL, LSM, Irish SL, many others
DGS: more independent origin but French influence via educational exchange
Both: one-handed manual alphabet (shared French tradition)
NOTATION COMPARISON
Both: SignWriting and HamNoSys used for research
Both: glossing in respective national spoken languages
Both: one-handed manual alphabet (important shared feature)
LSF: parent • DGS: sibling/descendant — both shaped by Enlightenment
PLATE 64a ALT · LSF / DGS · NOTATION
